items.q: Why did the League of Nations fail to keep peace?
items.correct: It lacked an army, the USA never joined, and it could not enforce decisions, so aggressors like Italy and Japan defied it.
items.misconception: The League failed because it had too much military power.
items.random_wrong: The League of Nations caused the Second World War directly.
items.why: Students misjudge its weakness; the problem was lack of enforcement and key members, not excess strength.

items.q: Did the Treaty of Versailles cause lasting peace?
items.correct: No — its harsh terms fed German resentment that the Nazis exploited, contributing to the instability that led to the Second World War.
items.misconception: Versailles created a stable and lasting European peace.
items.why: Learners assume the treaty worked; its severity and the resentment it bred are widely seen as helping cause the next war.

items.q: Did the superpowers fight each other directly in the Cold War?
items.correct: No — they avoided direct war, competing instead through proxy conflicts, an arms race and ideological rivalry.
items.misconception: The USA and USSR fought a direct full-scale war.
items.random_wrong: The Cold War was fought between Britain and Germany.
items.why: Students expect open war; the 'cold' label reflects rivalry without direct fighting between the two superpowers.

items.q: What did the Treaty of Versailles do to Germany in 1919?
items.correct: Imposed reparations, territorial loss and war guilt
items.random_wrong: Made it the dominant European power
items.why: It's Imposed reparations, territorial loss and war guilt. “Rewarded it with new colonies” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Why was the League of Nations weak?
items.correct: The USA never joined and it could not stop aggression
items.misconception: It had a huge standing army
items.why: Correct answer: The USA never joined and it could not stop aggression. “It had a huge standing army” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what was the Cold War a conflict between?
items.correct: The capitalist USA and communist USSR
items.why: It's The capitalist USA and communist USSR. “Britain and France” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What event in 1989 symbolised the collapse of communism in Europe?
items.correct: The fall of the Berlin Wall
items.why: It's The fall of the Berlin Wall. “The Treaty of Versailles” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was the Treaty of Versailles signed?
items.why: Exact answer: 1919 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
